revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥海修験]]
[[File:Mt Chokai from Kawauti.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|qid=Q1595289|lt=Sacred mountain|1=Sacred mountains}} Mount Chōkai]] '''Chōkai Shugendō''' (鳥海修験) refers to the practices of {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} carried out on {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|qid=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai|1=Mount Chōkai}}, which spans {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} and {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}. Since ancient times, Mount Chōkai has been regarded as a sacred mountain and a deity in itself, with Shugendō practices evolving around its various trailheads. == Overview == This article provides an explanation of Chōkai Shugendō, incorporating an overview of the general practices of {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} (see {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} for more details). Shugendō is said to have been founded by {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|qid=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} (also known as En no Ozunu). According to *Chōkai-san Shinkō-shi* (鳥海山信仰史)<ref name="shinkoushi">Matsumoto Ryōichi, *Chōkai-san Shinkō-shi*, Hon no Kai, July 1984.</ref>, En no Gyōja identified tranquil, pristine places, especially grand mountains, as abodes of deities and made them sites for ascetic practices. There are two modes of Shugendō training: '''Junpō''' (順峯, "ascending the front") and '''Gyakuhō''' (逆峯, "descending the reverse"). *Junpō* refers to ascetic training that starts from {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Wakayama Prefecture), ascends to Mount Ōmine, and ends at {{ill|Mount Yoshino|en|Mount Yoshino|qid=Q1036938|lt=Yoshino Mountain|1=Mount Yoshino}} (Nara Prefecture). Conversely, *Gyakuhō* involves starting from Yoshino, ascending Mount Ōmine, and finishing at Kumano. According to *Chōkai-san Shinkō-shi*<ref name="shinkoushi" />, Shugendō is divided into two main sects: * '''Honzan-ha''': Associated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tiantai}} (main temple: {{ill|Shōgo-in|en|Shōgo-in|qid=Q8664338|lt=Shōgo-in Temple|1=Shōgo-in}}), Kumano sect, and Junpō. * '''Tōzan-ha''': Associated with {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} (main temple: {{ill|Sanbō-in|en|Sanbō-in|qid=Q3471686|lt=Sanbō-in|1=Sanbō-in}}), Yoshino sect, and Gyakuhō. In the case of Mount Chōkai, sectarian affiliation and the choice between Junpō and Gyakuhō do not always align with these classifications. As noted in *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1* (出羽三山と修験道 戸川安章著作集Ⅰ)<ref name="sanzan">Togawa Anshō, *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1*, Iwata Shoin, February 2005.</ref>, early trailheads were considered "front entrances," while later ones were "rear entrances," with Junpō associated with the former and Gyakuhō with the latter. Additionally, *Chōkai-san Shi* (鳥海山史)<ref name="sanshi">Anesaki Iwakurō, *Chōkai-san Shi*, Kokusho Kankōkai, December 1983.</ref> introduces the notion that spring training was called Junpō and autumn training Gyakuhō, as per the *Yashima Historical Tales*. Since ancient times, Mount Chōkai was revered as a divine mountain. Under the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto), the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honji Suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} was adopted. Here, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (Bhaiṣajyaguru) manifested as the Ōmonoimi deity of Mount Chōkai to aid the people, leading to the emergence of Chōkai-san Daigongen. As a result, Mount Chōkai became a site of ascetic practices, with key trailheads such as {{ill|Yashima|en|Yashima|qid=Q415121|lt=Yashima|1=Yashima}}, {{ill|Kotaki|ja|小滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukura|ja|吹浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Warabioka|ja|蕨岡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabioka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} attracting {{ill|Yamabushi|en|Yamabushi|qid=Q2309838|lt=yamabushi|1=Yamabushi}} (Shugendō practitioners). According to *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1*<ref name="sanzan" />, Yakushi Nyorai was regarded as the "true form," and {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|qid=Q2329261|qid=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}} as the "trace deity" (垂迹神). Since ancient times, {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|qid=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai|1=Mount Chōkai}} has been revered as a sacred mountain. With the introduction of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and the doctrine of {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honji Suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}}, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (Bhaiṣajyaguru, specifically Yakushi Rurikō Nyorai) was identified as the divine manifestation of the Ōmonoimi deity residing in Japan's Mount Chōkai, said to bring salvation to the people. From this premise, Chōkai-san Daigongen emerged, transforming Mount Chōkai into a revered site of ascetic practice. Major trailheads such as {{ill|Yashima|en|Yashima|qid=Q415121|lt=Yashima|1=Yashima}}, {{ill|Kotaki|ja|小滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukura|ja|吹浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Warabioka|ja|蕨岡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabioka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} became hubs for {{ill|Yamabushi|en|Yamabushi|qid=Q2309838|lt=yamabushi|1=Yamabushi}} (Shugendō practitioners). According to *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1* (出羽三山と修験道 戸川安章著作集Ⅰ)<ref name="sanzan">Togawa Anshō, *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1*, Iwata Shoin, February 2005.</ref>, Yakushi Rurikō Nyorai was regarded as the "true form," while {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|qid=Q2329261|qid=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}} was considered the "trace deity." According to *Yamagata-ken Shi: Tsūshi-hen Dai 1-kan, Genshi-Kodai-Chūsei-hen* (山形県史 通史編第1巻 原始・古代・中世編)<ref name="tsuuzoku">Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata-ken Shi: Tsūshi-hen Dai 1-kan, Genshi-Kodai-Chūsei-hen*, Yamagata Prefecture, March 1979.</ref>, Warabioka became a key center for Chōkai Shugendō around the time a {{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}} (temple-shrine complex) was established in Fukura<ref>*{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}*, entry dated 21st day of the 11th month, first year of Ninwa (885), confirms the existence of a Jingū-ji in Akumi District, suggesting its foundation predates this record.</ref>. *Chōkai-san Shi* (鳥海山史)<ref name="sanshi">Anesaki Iwakurō, *Chōkai-san Shi*, Kokusho Kankōkai, December 1983.</ref> posits that the Shugendō traditions in Yashima predate those in Fukura and Warabioka, but the precise history of the development of mandalic representations of the mountain, the establishment of ascetic routes, and when practitioners began settling at the trailheads remains unclear due to the lack of historical records. Over time, competition and conflicts arose among the practitioners at different trailheads. *Chōkai-san Shinkō-shi*<ref name="shinkoushi">Matsumoto Ryōichi, *Chōkai-san Shinkō-shi*, Hon no Kai, July 1984.</ref> introduces a statement from the *Akumi District Gazetteer* (飽海郡誌), asserting that Fukura emphasized rituals at the shrine front, venerating the Ōmonoimi deity, while Warabioka, as the academic and administrative head of Chōkai-san Daigongen, served the mountain summit directly, holding overarching authority over the entire mountain. The book considers this account credible. Further analysis of inscriptions on ridge tags and other records indicates that the management and guidance of ascetic pilgrims climbing Mount Chōkai were carried out by practitioners from the three main trailheads—Yashima, Kotaki, and Warabioka. In contrast, Fukura's practitioners relied primarily on traditional shrine-related services, such as performing esoteric rituals at the shrine precincts, to sustain their income. According to *Chōkai-san Shi*<ref name="sanshi" />, conflicts between the {{ill|Honzan-ha|ja|本山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzan-ha|1=Honzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11520192}} (Tendai sect) and the {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487826}} (Shingon sect) escalated significantly, prompting the {{ill|Edo Bakufu|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Bakufu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to issue the Shugendō Statute in the 18th year of Keichō (1613) to establish order. However, this effort failed, and the shogunate later mandated affiliation with either the Honzan-ha or Tōzan-ha sects. Further, as noted in *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1*<ref name="sanzan" />, the shogunate implemented a policy in the 19th year of Keichō (1614) elevating the Tendai sect as the foremost religious authority. *Chōkai-san Shi*<ref name="sanshi" /> explains that while the {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|qid=Q1155726|lt=Three Mountains of Dewa|1=Three Mountains of Dewa}} adopted the Tendai sect en masse to maintain independence, Mount Chōkai remained divided between the Honzan-ha and Tōzan-ha sects due to the differing affiliations of its trailheads. The book highlights an example from the Edo period: disputes between Shugendō practitioners from Yashima and Warabioka escalated into the {{ill|Yashima-Honjin Boundary Dispute|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社#蕨岡、矢島の嶺境の論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-Honjin Boundary Dispute|1=Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11173679}}, involving the Yashima Domain and the Shōnai Domain. The book argues that this conflict was not merely territorial but reflected the competing historical claims and distinct sectarian affiliations of the involved parties, further intensifying the disputes. In the first year of Meiji (1868), the new government issued the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Bunri Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=Shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1970535}}, followed by the Shugendō Ban Decree in the fifth year of Meiji (1872). These decrees eradicated Shugendō practices on Mount Chōkai, and Shugendō practitioners vanished entirely. *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1*<ref name="sanzan" /> notes that more than a century after the decline of Chōkai Shugendō, the loss of practitioners and scattered historical materials have made its study extraordinarily challenging.
== Trailheads of Chōkai Shugendō == * Fukura Trailhead : Location: {{ill|Yuza, Akumi District, Yamagata|ja|山形県飽海郡遊佐町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuza, Akumi District, Yamagata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Shrine Name: {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|en|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|qid=Q11173679|lt=Chōkai-san Ōmonoimi Shrine|1=Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine}} : Temple Guardian (Betto): Jingū-ji : Ascetic Route: Junpū (順峯, ascending order) : With the northward expansion of Dewa Province's administrative center, the deities of Mount Gassan and Ōmonoimi were enshrined in Akumi District, leading to the establishment of Jingū-ji, marking the origin of the Fukura Trailhead. According to the *Akumi District Gazetteer*, there were 25 temples and 3 shrine families. During the "Ox King Seal Dispute" with Warabioka, a document exchanged in the first year of Meireki (1655) indicated that Fukura had originally followed the {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai sect|1=Tiantai}} but later transitioned to the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect|1=Shingon Buddhism}}. When Mount Haguro adopted the Tendai sect entirely, Fukura returned to Tendai in the 3rd year of Keian (1650). *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1*<ref name="sanzan" /> mentions that Fukura eventually reverted to the Shingon sect during the Hōei period when it became a branch of Goji-in<ref>Merged with Gokoku-ji during the Meiji period.</ref>. Unlike Warabioka, Fukura had weaker Shugendō practices, emphasizing service at the shrine front. Consequently, while practitioners at other trailheads ascended Mount Chōkai for promotion, Fukura practitioners climbed Mount Haguro, receiving appointments there. * Warabioka Trailhead : Location: {{ill|Yuza, Akumi District, Yamagata|ja|山形県飽海郡遊佐町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuza, Akumi District, Yamagata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Shrine Name: Chōkai-san Ōmonoimi Shrine : Temple Guardian (Betto): {{ill|Ryūzu-ji (Yuza)|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzu-ji (Yuza)|1=Ryūtō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11679435}} : Ascetic Route: Junpū (順峯, ascending order) : The exact timing of Shugendō's introduction to Warabioka is unknown. However, it absorbed two Shugendō lodges in Sugisawa Village that held the keys to the Yakushi Hall (now the mountain summit shrine of Chōkai-san Ōmonoimi Shrine). By the Edo period, it had grown into a major base with 33 lodges (or 32). According to the "Ox King Seal Dispute" document from the first year of Meireki (1655), Warabioka had always been affiliated with the Shingon sect, which would classify it under the Yoshino faction (reverse ascent). Nevertheless, Warabioka practitioners referred to their ascent as Junpū (ascending order). In the first year of Meireki (1655), when attending a Tendai-style memorial service for {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|qid=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} at Nikko Mountain, Warabioka maintained its Shingon affiliation despite being under the administration of Mount Haguro. In the 1st year of Jōkyō (1684), Warabioka seceded from Mount Haguro, affiliating itself with {{ill|Daigo Sanbō-in|ja|醍醐三宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigo Sanbō-in|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三宝院|1=Sanbō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3471686}}. * Kotaki Trailhead : Location: {{ill|Nikaho, Akita|ja|秋田県にかほ市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikaho, Akita|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Shrine Name: {{ill|Kinpō Shrine (Nikaho)|ja|金峰神社 (にかほ市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpō Shrine (Nikaho)|1=Kinpō Shrine (Nikaho City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11647033}} : Temple Guardian (Betto): Ryūzan-ji : Ascetic Route: Junpū (順峯, ascending order) : By the mid-Edo period, Kotaki had nine Shugendō lodges. According to *Chōkai-san Shinkō-shi*<ref name="shinkoushi" />, a document addressed to Ryūzan-ji on the 5th day of the 7th month of the 5th year of Jōkyō (1688) indicates that Kotaki followed the Shingon sect under Daigo Sanbō-in. * Takizawa Trailhead : Location: {{ill|Yurihonjō, Akita|ja|秋田県由利本荘市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurihonjō, Akita|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Shrine Name: Moriko Ōmonoimi Shrine : Temple Guardian (Betto): Ryūdō-ji : Ascetic Route: Gyakupū (逆峯, reverse ascent) : During the Keichō era, a conflict erupted between the Takizawa and Yashima Trailheads regarding the reverse ascent Tōzan faction. A document from the 14th year of Genroku (1701) references further disputes. According to *Chōkai-san Shi*<ref name="sanshi" />, the absence of Ryūdō-ji in local histories suggests Takizawa's decline following its defeat in disputes with Yashima. * Yashima Trailhead : Location: {{ill|Yurihonjō, Akita|ja|秋田県由利本荘市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurihonjō, Akita|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Shrine Name: Kibisaka Ōmonoimi Shrine : Temple Guardian (Betto): Fukuō-ji : Ascetic Route: Gyakupū (逆峯, reverse ascent) : Based on *Chōkai-san Shi*<ref name="sanshi" />, the Yashima Trailhead ascetic path was developed in the 3rd year of Kashō (850) by brothers Hirae and Tarae from {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}. A document from the 6th year of Enpō (1678) confirms that Yashima practitioners were affiliated with the reverse-ascent Tōzan Shingon faction since its foundation. The founder is said to be {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11610974}}, who opened the route in the 12th year of Jōgan (870). By the mid-Edo period, Yashima had 18 Shugendō lodges. According to *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Togawa Anshō Vol. 1*<ref name="sanzan" />, inscriptions on mountain-top ridge tags indicate that Yashima surpassed Takizawa in importance during the early Edo period. * Innai Trailhead : Location: Nikaho, Akita : Shrine Name: Shichikō Shrine : Temple Guardian (Betto): Gokuraku-ji : According to *Chōkai-san Shi*<ref name="sanshi" />, while Innai likely followed Shugendō, a document addressed to the lord of Nikaho in the 2nd year of Genroku (1689) stated that Gokuraku-ji was under the jurisdiction of Kōyasan's Shin'nan-in<ref>Now Fumon-in.</ref>. This affiliation suggests that Innai had a different lineage from the Yashima Trailhead. Gokuraku-ji is said to have declined in the early modern period. == References == <references />{{Reflist|group=note|qq=}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukigawa River|ja|月光川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukigawa River|1=Gakkō River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11515864}} * Matsubadera (Located in Meguro, Yuza Town, Akumi District, Yamagata Prefecture. This temple took over the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha|1=Honji suijaku}} of Ōmonoimi ({{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}) from Fukura Trailhead and the honji-butsu of Tsukuyomi ({{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|qid=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}}), adopting the mountain name of Chōkai.) == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroita Katsumi|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroita Katsumi|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3894478}} and the Kokushi Taikei Editorial Committee, *{{ill|Kokushi Taikei|ja|国史大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Taikei|1=Kokushi Taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10926780}} Vol. 4: Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*. Tokyo: {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}}, April 1966. * Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History: Materials Edition, Vol. 5: Keirōku Part 1*. Yamagata Prefecture, May 1961. * Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History: Materials Edition, Vol. 6: Keirōku Part 2*. Yamagata Prefecture, November 1961. * Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History: General Edition, Vol. 1: Ancient and Medieval*. Yamagata Prefecture, March 1979. * Tetsu Anzai, Kensuke Hashimoto, and Masami Abe, *Yamagata Local Studies Series Vol. 7: Famous Spots of Mount Chōkai*. Tokyo: {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420946}}, November 1982. (Reprint of 1931 publication by the Yamagata Prefectural Local Studies Association.) * Iwakura Anesaki, *Chōkai-san History*. Tokyo: {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420946}}, December 1983. * Ryōichi Matsumoto, *Chōkai-san Faith History*. Tokyo: Hon no Kai, July 1984. * Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office 50th Anniversary Committee, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Register*. Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, April 2000. * {{ill|Anshō Togawa|ja|戸川安章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anshō Togawa|1=Anshō Togawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11496355}}, *Dewa Sanzan and Shugendō: The Collected Works of Anshō Togawa Vol. 1*. Tokyo: Iwata Shoin, February 2005. {{Buddhism2|qq=}}
{{Omonoimi Faith|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|鳥海修験|version=93027290|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:鳥海修験]]

{{wikidata link|Q11675024}} [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:History of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:History of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥海山]]
